lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Vouchsafe to those that have not read the story,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: That I may prompt them: and of such as have,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: I humbly pray them to admit the excuse

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Of time, of numbers and due course of things,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Which cannot in their huge and proper life

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Be here presented. Now we bear the king

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Toward Calais: grant him there; there seen,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Heave him away upon your winged thoughts

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Athwart the sea. Behold, the English beach

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Pales in the flood with men, with wives and boys,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Whose shouts and claps out-voice the deep-mouth’d sea,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Which like a mighty whiffler ’fore the king

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Seems to prepare his way: so let him land,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: And solemnly see him set on to London.

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: So swift a pace hath thought that even now

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: You may imagine him upon Blackheath;

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Where that his lords desire him to have borne

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: His bruised helmet and his bended sword

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Before him through the city: he forbids it,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Being free from vainness and self-glorious pride;

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Giving full trophy, signal and ostent

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Quite from himself to God. But now behold,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: In the quick forge and working-house of thought,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: How London doth pour out her citizens!

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: The mayor and all his brethren in best sort,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Like to the senators of the antique Rome,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: With the plebeians swarming at their heels,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Go forth and fetch their conquering Caesar in:

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: As, by a lower but loving likelihood,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Were now the general of our gracious empress,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: As in good time he may, from Ireland coming,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Bringing rebellion broached on his sword,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: How many would the peaceful city quit,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: To welcome him! much more, and much more cause,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Did they this Harry. Now in London place him;

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: As yet the lamentation of the French

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Invites the King of England’s stay at home;

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: The emperor’s coming in behalf of France,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: To order peace between them; and omit

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: All the occurrences, whatever chanced,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Till Harry’s back-return again to France:

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: There must we bring him; and myself have play’d

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: The interim, by remembering you ’tis past.

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Then brook abridgement, and your eyes advance,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: After your thoughts, straight back again to France.

glosses.definition: grant permission; be so good as to allow

glosses.phrase: of time, of numbers and due course of things
glosses.definition: constraints of time, population size, and the proper sequence of events — the Chorus apologizes for compression
Henry's staging port for the return crossing

glosses.phrase: Heave him away upon your winged thoughts
glosses.definition: lift him on your imagination's wings
across the sea — the English Channel
encloses the flood with a fence of people — the crowds line the shore
drown out the booming sea
an armed official who cleared the way in processions — 'whiffler' = herald/marshal
an open area south-east of London where royal entries traditionally assembled

glosses.phrase: bruised helmet and his bended sword
glosses.definition: Henry's battered Agincourt arms — which his lords want to parade
Henry refuses the triumphal display — his modesty claim
vanity and boastful pride
show and outward display

glosses.phrase: quick forge and working-house of thought
glosses.definition: the active smithy of the mind — the Chorus's image for rapid imaginative scene-change
the Lord Mayor of London and his aldermen
senators of ancient Rome — the Chorus elevates London's civic reception to a Roman triumph
common people — completes the Roman parallel
Henry as returning Caesar — the triumphal image
a humbler but affectionate parallel — the Chorus draws a current analogy
the Earl of Essex — Queen Elizabeth's favorite, then leading troops in Ireland; the play's rare topical reference, datable to spring 1599
rebellion impaled on his sword — the hoped-for Irish campaign victory that did not actually happen
imagine Henry in London now

glosses.phrase: emperor’s coming in behalf of France
glosses.definition: Emperor Sigismund came to England (1416) to mediate peace — a historical episode the Chorus skips over
everything that happened — omitted for compression
Henry's second invasion of France (1417–1420), which led to the Treaty of Troyes
acted (out) the intervening years — the Chorus as time-compressor
tolerate the shortening

annotations.title: London as Rome — the triumphal reception
annotations.body: The 5 Chorus describes Henry's return to London in deliberately Roman terms: the mayor and aldermen 'Like to the senators of the antique Rome,' the plebeians at their heels, going forth to fetch their 'conquering Caesar in.' Henry's refusal of the triumphal display — refusing to have his bruised helmet and bent sword carried before him — is registered as humility ('being free from vainness and self-glorious pride'). Readers should note the layered strategy: the Chorus Latinizes London, elevates the event to antique ceremony, and then has Henry decline the ceremony, giving him all the moral credit of humility while retaining all the classical grandeur the framing confers. This is rhetorical double-counting. The passage is also the point at which the Chorus's voice sounds most frankly adulatory — and critics have often read this as the moment Shakespeare's irony is least available. Whether one hears the Chorus-stanza as sincere or as over-freighted with praise is a test of how the whole play sounds to each reader.
sources: Andrew Gurr (ed.), King Henry V (New Cambridge Shakespeare, 1992)

annotations.title: The Essex reference — the play's one dating anchor
annotations.body: 'Were now the general of our gracious empress, / As in good time he may, from Ireland coming, / Bringing rebellion broached on his sword' — this is Henry V's only unambiguous topical reference, and its dating is important. The 'general of our gracious empress' is the Earl of Essex, Queen Elizabeth I's favorite, who set out for Ireland in late March 1599 to put down Hugh O'Neill's rebellion. Essex returned in September 1599 without victory; his attempted rebellion and execution followed in 1601. This means Henry V was written and performed in the narrow window between roughly March and September 1599 — almost certainly for the opening of the Globe that summer. The reference is optimistic about Essex ('as in good time he may') in a way that became impossible after his return. The line is thus historical evidence about both the play's composition date and the political sympathies of the Chamberlain's Men in the weeks before the Essex disaster. By the time the Folio was printed in 1623, Essex was twenty years dead; the line read (and reads) as the ghost of a hope.
sources: Gary Taylor (ed.), The Oxford Shakespeare: Henry V (1982), introduction
Paul E. J. Hammer, The Polarisation of Elizabethan Politics (1999)
Andrew Gurr (ed.), King Henry V (New Cambridge Shakespeare, 1992)

trials.prompt: How does the Chorus describe Henry's return to London (lines 9–28)?
options: In Roman triumphal terms — the mayor and aldermen as 'senators of the antique Rome,' Londoners as plebeians, Henry as 'conquering Caesar' — but Henry declines the triumphal display, gaining the credit of humility while retaining the classical grandeur the framing supplies
As a secret arrival by night

trials.prompt: The reference at lines 29–34 to 'the general of our gracious empress … from Ireland coming' dates the play how?
options: To late March–September 1599 — during the Earl of Essex's Irish campaign, while hope for his success was still possible. After his return without victory (autumn 1599) and his execution (1601), the line could not have been written in that form
To 1595, before the Globe was built
